topic: Two siblings inherit their grandmother's house
raw_output:
[story 1]

The solicitor’s voice echoed in the cavernous entryway, a sound that seemed to amplify the sheer weight of the inheritance pressing down on me. I clutched the brittle parchment, my knuckles white against the aged paper. My older brother, Mark, was already halfway up the grand staircase, whistling a jaunty tune that grated on my nerves. I felt a flush creep up my neck, hot and undeniable. This house—Grandmother’s house—was a monument to everything I’d ever secretly resented about her, and now it was mine. I took a shallow breath, trying to smooth the sudden tremor in my hands. "Well," I managed, my voice coming out thin and reedy, "this is… a lot to process." I wanted the floor to swallow me whole.

[story 2]

I stood paralyzed in the drawing-room, the silence of the old manor thick enough to choke on. The sunlight, filtering through the dusty lace curtains, illuminated every speck of grime, making the room feel suddenly exposed. Mark was already examining the antique furniture with an air of practiced ease, running a finger across a mahogany armoire. I kept my gaze fixed on a chipped porcelain figurine on the mantelpiece, focusing intently on its hairline crack. Every muscle in my body felt tight, coiled in a strange, unwelcome tension. When Mark finally turned, his smile didn't reach his eyes, and I could feel the heat rising in my cheeks. "So," he said, his tone overly casual, "we're stuck with it." I swallowed hard, the dryness in my throat making the action painful.

[story 3]

The sheer scale of the place overwhelmed me. I trailed my fingers along the carved banister, the wood cool and strangely resonant beneath my touch. Every shadow seemed to deepen, turning the familiar rooms into something vast and intimidating. I tried to walk forward, to take charge, but my feet felt rooted to the spot. I could hear the faint creak of the old floorboards protesting under my weight, and a strange, tight feeling constricted my chest. When my sister, Clara, finally approached, offering a tentative, "Let's start with the library," I could only manage a jerky nod. My mouth felt suddenly dry, and I instinctively pulled my sweater tighter around myself, a futile attempt to shield myself from the scrutiny of the overwhelming space.

The sheer scale of the place overwhelmed me. I trailed my fingers along the carved banister, the wood cool and strangely resonant beneath my touch.
